name: National Security Framing
consequences: Reframing platform dependency as a national security risk elevates urgency in policy circles. It may trigger stricter procurement scrutiny but could also provoke pushback from tech incumbents and increase political polarization. The heightened focus may attract additional funding, yet risks overregulation and potential delays in decision-making.
options: Launch a high‑profile briefing series for ministers and parliamentarians that quantifies the strategic risk of platform lock‑in and proposes a national security mandate for platform neutrality.
Publish a peer‑reviewed white paper linking platform dependency to cyber resilience metrics and circulate it through defense and intelligence agencies to build cross‑sector consensus.
Organize a national cyber resilience summit that brings together academia, industry, and civil society to co‑create a security charter demanding platform‑neutral authentication pathways.
review: Framing the issue as national security raises political stakes but may sideline privacy concerns and leave the technical feasibility of fallback paths unaddressed.

name: EU Procurement Mandate
consequences: Leveraging EU procurement rules to require platform neutrality forces suppliers to design for multiple OS, reducing long‑term vendor lock‑in. It may increase upfront costs and slow procurement cycles, but it also creates a regulatory baseline that future contracts must follow. Compliance burdens may rise for small vendors.
options: Draft a model procurement specification that embeds platform‑neutrality clauses and submit it to the European Commission for endorsement as a best‑practice template.
Coordinate with the Danish procurement authority to pilot a competitive tender that rewards vendors offering certified fallback authentication on non‑Apple/Google platforms.
Advocate for inclusion of platform‑neutrality language in the EU Digital Services Act, thereby creating a regulatory baseline that Danish contracts must follow.
review: Mandating platform neutrality in procurement can delay contract awards and increase vendor costs, yet it does not guarantee that fallback mechanisms will be technically robust.

name: Fallback Authentication Standardization
consequences: Establishing a formal standard for fallback authentication provides a clear technical blueprint for public services. It requires coordination with multiple stakeholders and may face resistance from existing identity operators who prefer proprietary solutions. A new compliance layer could slow adoption.
options: Lead the creation of an open‑source fallback authentication protocol that integrates FIDO2/WebAuthn with eIDAS token formats and publish it under a permissive license.
Form a cross‑sector working group that negotiates a binding interoperability agreement for fallback authentication, ensuring that all public portals can accept alternative credentials.
Develop a modular fallback authentication SDK that can be embedded into existing portals, allowing rapid deployment without full system redesign.
review: Standardizing fallback authentication offers a clear technical path but may create a new compliance burden and could be ignored by legacy systems.

name: National Testbed Creation
consequences: Building a secure, controlled test environment for platform‑neutral identity experiments enables rapid prototyping and risk assessment. It demands significant upfront investment in infrastructure and governance, and may face regulatory hurdles for data handling. The testbed may also limit external participation due to security constraints.
options: Secure a dedicated government lab space in Copenhagen and equip it with GrapheneOS devices, hardware tokens, and sandboxed identity servers for iterative testing.
Partner with a university cyber security center to co‑host a shared testbed that offers isolated environments for both mobile and web fallback demonstrations.
Establish a cloud‑based virtual testbed that mimics the Danish public‑sector identity ecosystem, allowing remote stakeholders to run experiments under strict access controls.
review: Creating a national testbed accelerates experimentation but demands high capital and may restrict broader stakeholder engagement due to security constraints.

name: Alternative Distribution Channels
consequences: Developing non‑Apple/Google app distribution mechanisms reduces dependency on mainstream app stores. It may face legal challenges from platform owners, require user education, and could fragment the user experience. Distribution costs may rise and adoption could be uneven.
options: Launch a secure, signed‑over‑the‑air distribution portal that delivers identity apps directly to verified Danish citizens, bypassing mainstream app stores.
Collaborate with telecom operators to embed identity app packages into device firmware updates, ensuring pre‑installed access without third‑party stores.
Create a decentralized app marketplace using blockchain‑based identity verification, allowing users to download and install identity apps from a trusted peer network.
review: Alternative distribution channels cut store dependence but risk legal pushback and may fragment user experience, leaving the fallback path untested.

name: Certification Scheme for Platform‑Neutral Apps
consequences: Introducing a formal certification process for identity apps that meet platform‑neutral criteria incentivizes developers to adopt fallback‑friendly designs. It adds certification overhead, may slow app release, and could be perceived as regulatory overreach. A new market niche may emerge.
options: Design a certification framework that evaluates apps against platform‑neutral security metrics and publish a public registry of certified products.
Partner with an existing national cybersecurity authority to administer annual audits of identity apps, awarding certificates that guarantee fallback compatibility.
Establish a voluntary accreditation program that rewards developers with public recognition and procurement priority for meeting platform‑neutral standards.
review: Certification drives quality but adds administrative burden and may exclude smaller developers, leaving the fallback ecosystem underrepresented.

name: Supplier Concentration Risk Mitigation
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on Apple/Google by diversifying platform providers, but it may increase integration complexity and delay procurement cycles. It forces stakeholders to evaluate alternative OS ecosystems, potentially raising development costs and requiring new certification pathways.
options: Negotiate a multi‑vendor platform agreement that includes open‑source Android variants and a dedicated Danish OS, ensuring each vendor meets defined integrity signal standards.
Establish a vendor‑neutral certification board that audits any mobile OS for compliance with eIDAS and NSIS, allowing future suppliers to enter without legacy lock‑in.
Create a sandboxed micro‑kernel environment that isolates identity apps from underlying OS, permitting any vendor to host the app while preserving integrity checks.
review: Diversifying vendors increases integration overhead and certification complexity, yet the options still leave the challenge of aligning disparate OS security models with existing MitID trust assumptions unaddressed.

name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Development
consequences: Implementing a fallback path provides continuity during platform outages, but introduces additional authentication steps that may degrade user experience and require new policy approvals. It also demands secure key management outside the current mobile OS ecosystem.
options: Deploy a hardware‑token based fallback that uses NFC‑enabled key fobs, allowing citizens to authenticate via a dedicated reader at public service kiosks.
Introduce a browser‑based WebAuthn flow that leverages the user's existing desktop browser, bypassing mobile OS dependencies while maintaining strong cryptographic guarantees.
Implement a short‑lived QR‑code challenge that pairs a mobile device with a secondary device, enabling cross‑platform authentication without relying on OS integrity signals.
review: Adding fallback mechanisms adds procedural friction and requires new security controls, yet the options do not fully resolve the need for a seamless, low‑friction user experience during platform disruptions.

name: Platform‑Neutral Procurement Language
consequences: Embedding platform‑neutral clauses in procurement documents forces future identity solutions to support multiple OS ecosystems, but may slow down tender processes and increase initial development costs. It also risks alienating incumbent vendors who rely on platform exclusivity.
options: Draft procurement specifications that mandate support for at least two distinct mobile OS families, with explicit testing for integrity signal compliance.
Introduce a phased procurement approach where early pilots must demonstrate platform neutrality before full contract award.
Require vendors to provide open‑source code for critical authentication modules, ensuring transparency and enabling cross‑platform adaptation.
review: Mandating platform neutrality raises procurement complexity and vendor costs, yet the options leave the issue of how to enforce compliance during the tender evaluation unaddressed.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Expansion
consequences: Broadening the coalition increases advocacy power and resource sharing, but may dilute focus and create coordination challenges. It also risks conflicting priorities among diverse groups, potentially slowing decision‑making.
options: Form a cross‑sector task force that includes civil‑society, academia, and industry, meeting quarterly to align on resilience metrics and policy drafts.
Launch a public consultation campaign that solicits feedback from end‑users on fallback authentication preferences, feeding data into policy proposals.
Partner with neighboring Nordic governments to create a shared platform‑neutral identity research hub, pooling expertise and reducing duplication.
review: Expanding coalition breadth enhances influence but introduces coordination overhead, while the options do not address how to reconcile divergent stakeholder agendas during policy drafting.

name: Technical Demonstrator Validation
consequences: Building demonstrators validates technical feasibility and provides evidence for policy arguments, but consumes significant development resources and may divert focus from policy work. Demonstrators also risk being perceived as proof‑of‑concept rather than production‑ready, limiting stakeholder buy‑in.
options: Construct a GrapheneOS‑based mobile demonstrator that authenticates via FIDO2/WebAuthn against a simulated public‑sector identity provider.
Develop a browser‑based fallback demonstrator that uses OpenID Connect with a hardware‑token backed key store, showcasing cross‑platform capability.
Create a hybrid demonstrator that combines NFC key fobs with a lightweight server, illustrating end‑to‑end fallback authentication flow.
review: Demonstrator development validates concepts but consumes resources that could support policy engagement, and the options still leave the scalability of the solutions to real‑world deployment unaddressed.

name: Regulatory Framing of Resilience
consequences: Reframing the issue as a resilience requirement shifts policy focus from platform preference to continuity, potentially easing procurement constraints but may trigger stricter regulatory scrutiny and longer approval timelines. It could also lead to increased compliance costs for vendors.
options: Draft a resilience‑centric policy brief that quantifies downtime risks from platform lock‑in and recommends mandatory fallback provisions.
Propose a regulatory amendment that classifies platform neutrality as a core resilience criterion for all public‑sector digital identity contracts.
Advocate for a national resilience certification that identity solutions must obtain, covering both platform‑neutral and fallback capabilities.
review: Framing as resilience may reduce vendor lock‑in but invites tighter regulation, while the options do not fully address how to balance regulatory burden against operational flexibility.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Aligning with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards enhances interoperability and may attract broader support, but could impose additional compliance obligations and delay national implementation. It also risks aligning Denmark with standards that still favor platform dominance.
options: Map Danish fallback requirements onto the EU Digital Identity Wallet architecture, identifying gaps and proposing extensions for platform neutrality.
Participate in EU working groups to influence the next iteration of the Digital Identity Wallet, embedding fallback mechanisms in the core specification.
Develop a cross‑border pilot that demonstrates fallback authentication using EU‑approved identity wallets, showcasing interoperability benefits.
review: EU alignment expands interoperability but may delay national rollout, and the options leave the challenge of reconciling EU‑level standards with Denmark’s specific platform‑neutral goals unaddressed.

name: Cross-Platform Credential Interoperability
consequences: Pulling this lever would push for a unified credential format that can be validated across iOS, Android, and alternative OSes, reducing reliance on platform-specific integrity signals. This may increase initial integration complexity for existing services and require coordination with multiple vendors, potentially delaying rollout. Downstream, services can accept credentials from any device, but the need for a common schema may expose new attack surfaces if not hardened.
options: Develop a standardized credential schema that maps existing MitID assertions to a platform-agnostic token format, enabling cross-device verification without platform APIs.
Collaborate with major OS vendors to embed a lightweight credential validation layer that accepts the new schema, ensuring backward compatibility with current app stores.
Publish an open-source reference implementation of the credential verifier, allowing third parties to integrate the schema into their authentication flows without vendor involvement.
review: Pushing for cross-platform credentials raises integration overhead for existing services and may expose new attack vectors, yet it leaves the underlying platform integrity checks unaddressed, potentially limiting security guarantees.

name: Regulatory Incentive Alignment
consequences: Pulling this lever would create financial or regulatory incentives for public sector agencies to adopt platform-neutral solutions, potentially accelerating deployment. However, it may create a perception of favoritism toward certain vendors or technologies, leading to political pushback. Downstream, agencies might prioritize incentive-eligible projects over other critical improvements, skewing resource allocation.
options: Introduce a tax credit for agencies that integrate fallback authentication modules into their digital services, reducing the cost barrier for platform-neutral adoption.
Mandate that procurement contracts include a clause rewarding early compliance with platform-neutral standards, thereby creating a market signal for vendors to support the initiative.
Offer a grant program that funds pilot projects demonstrating the feasibility of platform-neutral authentication, encouraging experimentation without immediate cost to agencies.
review: Aligning incentives accelerates adoption but risks politicizing procurement and may divert resources from other priorities; the options do not directly mitigate the technical dependency on platform integrity signals.

name: Public-Private Security Consortium
consequences: Pulling this lever would establish a formal consortium of public agencies, private security firms, and academia to jointly develop and audit fallback authentication mechanisms. This could enhance trust and accelerate innovation but may introduce coordination overhead and confidentiality concerns, potentially slowing decision-making. Downstream, the consortium could produce shared threat models, but aligning diverse stakeholder incentives may be challenging.
options: Form a joint task force that includes representatives from the Ministry of Digital Affairs, leading cybersecurity firms, and university researchers to co-author a fallback authentication specification.
Create a secure, multi-party computation platform where consortium members can share threat intelligence on platform integrity vulnerabilities without exposing proprietary data.
Publish a set of best‑practice guidelines derived from consortium findings, making them publicly available to all Danish digital service providers.
review: A consortium fosters collaboration but adds coordination overhead and confidentiality risks; the options do not directly address the need for a platform-neutral fallback path in existing procurement cycles.

name: Hardware Token Adoption Pathway
consequences: Pulling this lever would promote the use of hardware tokens (e.g., YubiKey) as an alternative authentication factor, reducing reliance on mobile OS integrity signals. This may increase upfront costs for agencies and users, and require supply chain coordination, but could provide a more resilient authentication channel. Downstream: agencies may need to update their authentication workflows to accept token-based logins, potentially increasing complexity.
options: Develop a procurement framework that standardizes the specification and certification of hardware tokens for use in Danish public services, ensuring interoperability across agencies.
Launch a pilot program where selected municipalities issue hardware tokens to citizens for accessing health and tax portals, demonstrating feasibility and user experience.
Create an open-source token management portal that allows agencies to provision, revoke, and monitor token usage without relying on mobile OS infrastructure.
review: Promoting hardware tokens reduces OS dependency but raises procurement and user onboarding costs; the options do not mitigate the need for a fallback authentication path that can operate without any token infrastructure.

name: Open-Source Trust Anchor Development
consequences: Pulling this lever would create an open-source trust anchor that can validate device integrity without relying on Apple or Google APIs. This could democratize trust verification but may face skepticism from regulators and require rigorous security audits. Downstream: if adopted, services could rely on a community-maintained anchor, but the lack of commercial backing might limit widespread deployment.
options: Build a modular, open-source integrity verification library that can be embedded in any operating system, providing a platform-neutral trust anchor for authentication.
Establish a formal audit process with independent security firms to certify the library’s resilience against tampering and side‑channel attacks.
Publish a comprehensive developer guide that demonstrates how to integrate the library into existing authentication workflows, lowering the barrier for adoption.
review: An open-source trust anchor offers independence but may lack regulatory endorsement, and the options do not directly address how to embed it into current procurement processes.

name: Digital Identity Resilience Benchmarking
consequences: Pulling this lever would establish a set of resilience metrics and benchmarking exercises for Danish digital identity services, highlighting gaps in platform neutrality. This could drive policy changes but may require extensive data collection and could be perceived as an audit burden. Downstream: agencies may improve resilience but may also divert resources to meet benchmark targets.
options: Define a resilience scorecard that measures each service’s dependency on platform integrity signals, fallback availability, and incident response readiness.
Conduct a nationwide penetration test campaign that simulates platform outage scenarios, reporting findings to the Ministry of Digital Affairs for policy action.
Publish a public dashboard that tracks resilience metrics over time, enabling stakeholders to monitor progress and identify critical dependencies.
review: Benchmarking raises awareness but may impose audit overhead; the options do not directly provide a mechanism to enforce platform-neutral procurement.

name: Stakeholder-Driven Procurement Transparency
consequences: Pulling this lever would require procurement documents to disclose vendor lock‑in risks and platform dependency metrics, making transparency a decision criterion. This could pressure vendors to offer platform-neutral solutions but may slow procurement cycles and increase administrative burden. Downstream: agencies may choose more resilient vendors but may also face higher upfront costs.
options: Mandate that all public procurement tenders include a mandatory risk assessment section evaluating platform lock‑in and fallback capabilities.
Create a standardized procurement template that automatically flags vendors lacking platform-neutral certifications, ensuring early visibility during vendor selection.
Publish a comparative analysis of existing vendors’ platform dependency scores, making the information publicly available to inform procurement decisions.
review: Transparency pressures vendors but may delay procurement and increase paperwork; the options do not directly guarantee that platform-neutral solutions will be adopted.
